BBC

The Welsh towns turning their high streets into homes

By Iwan Lewis · BBC News, Cardiff

In the centre of Caerphilly, a former Woolworths has become eighteen one-bedroom flats. In Llanelli, the old Marks & Spencer is now a community café on the ground floor and twelve apartments above. Up the coast in Holyhead, a Wilko that closed in 2023 is being converted into a mix of social and affordable rented housing, due to open later this year.

It is part of a quiet but accelerating shift across Welsh high streets: the conversion of empty retail premises into homes. According to figures from the Welsh Government, more than 230 such conversions have been approved in the past eighteen months, up from 71 in the equivalent period before 2024.

Why now?

Two things changed. In late 2024 the Welsh Government simplified planning permission for change-of-use from retail to residential within designated town-centre zones, cutting an average eight-month process to under three months. At the same time, a £200m capital fund was opened for local authorities to either acquire and convert empty units themselves or to lend at concessionary rates to private developers willing to take on the work.

"We had a vacancy rate of 19 percent on Stepney Street," said Annette Probert, a councillor in Llanelli. "Now it's down to 9 percent, and most of the new tenants aren't shops. They're people."

Related stories

Not without trade-offs

Critics point to the loss of retail mix as a concern. "If you turn every empty shop into flats, you start to lose what a town centre is for," said Owain Watkins, who chairs the Welsh Federation of Independent Retailers. The Federation has called for a residential cap of 40 percent of any individual high street, with mixed-use schemes prioritised.

The Welsh Government says it will review the cap proposal in the next consultation round, but stresses that the alternative — leaving units empty — has been the dominant pattern for more than a decade and has hollowed out the very town centres people want to protect.

Mr Watkins concedes the point. "Empty isn't the answer. We just want to make sure 'full of flats' isn't either."
